sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Dizziness refers to a range of sensations including feeling light-headed, faint, woozy, unsteady or as if the surroundings are spinning. It is often caused by disruptions in the body’s balance system, which involves the inner ear, eyes, brain and nerves. In the prehospital setting, dizziness can be a symptom of a wide variety of conditions, from benign disorders to life-threatening emergencies. Common causes include dehydration, low blood pressure, inner ear disorders, medication side effects, and stress.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Sensations of spinning and visual disturbances
- Loss of balance, unsteady, weak
- Disorientation, confusion and difficulty concentrating
- Pallor and clamminess
- Nausea and/or vomiting
- Nystagmus

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Patients presenting with true vertigo should be managed under that clinical guideline
- Dizziness can appear insignificant on presentation but may have a serious underlying medical condition. A thorough assessment should be undertaken on all presentations of dizziness
- Dizziness can be caused by some of the following and should be managed accordingly:
  - Vertigo or BPPV/labyrinthitis/eardrum perforation/Meniere's disease
  - Stroke/TIA/migraine/brain tumours/Parkinson's disease/multiple sclerosis
  - Cervicogenic issues
  - ACS/arrhythmias
  - Alcohol withdrawal
  - Medication side effects
  - Sepsis
  - Dehydration/heat related illness
  - Hypoglycaemia
  - Anaemia
  - Postural issues
  - Anxiety/stress/depression
  - Recent trauma/pain
- Red flags:
  - Sudden onset of debilitating dizziness with associated neurological symptoms should raise the possibility of stroke
  - Sudden onset of dizziness with associated hearing loss and/or tinnitus
  - Sudden onset of dizziness with ear pain, discharge, trauma, or barotrauma requires medical review

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Position patient appropriately
- Oxygen if indicated
- Vital Sign Survey (Specifically pulse, blood pressure [postural BP], blood glucose and temperature)
- Conduct Secondary and neurological assessments (Cranial nerve assessment/CNS Survey/FAST)
- Urinalysis (specific gravity)

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Apply cardiac monitor
- Manage cardiac dysrhythmia
- 12 lead ECG should be acquired in most, if not all these patients
- Consider IV access if indicated
- Consider obtaining blood for i-STAT sampling (CG4+, CG8+)
- Consider fluid therapy
- Consider appropriate antiemetic

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Some pre-hospital procedures such as injections may precipitate dizziness in susceptible patients. Patient position should be considered to avoid potential injury
